entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: Skilled Technical (ST) composite ≥ 91, per two secondary sources (cogn-iq.org, study.com), consistent with the Army:ST composite listed for this cluster in data/interest-taxonomy.json. entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Army general accession standard; no 31B-specific deviation found).
entry_requirements.age_range: 17 (with parental consent) to 42, per Army's 2026 policy ceiling raised from 35 (data/branch-profiles.json). No 31B-specific age restriction found.
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Army accession medical and fitness standards, including the Army Fitness Test (AFT); no 31B-specific elevated physical standard was found beyond the general standard. Must hold or be eligible for a valid driver's license before reporting to training (per cogn-iq.org).
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Secret security clearance required, per multiple secondary sources (cogn-iq.org, operationmilitarykids.org). UNVERIFIED at primary-source level this session.
entry_requirements.other: Standard MP suitability screening excludes applicants with a history of drug/alcohol abuse or violence-related convictions — this pattern is consistent across every branch's MP/security-forces specialty documented in this file, not just Army.

training_pipeline.stage: One Station Unit Training (OSUT) — Military Police
training_pipeline.description: OSUT combines Basic Combat Training and MP Advanced Individual Training into one continuous course, reported at 19 weeks 1 day by one secondary source (rounded down to 19 here because this schema field is an integer). A second source separately describes an approximately 9-week AIT component and an 11-week figure for a related course; the sources were not fully reconcilable this session and the 19-week OSUT total (the more specific, more recently dated figure) is used here. Confirm exact current length with a recruiter or the Fort Leonard Wood MP School before publishing.

No current, verifiable enlistment or reenlistment bonus figure specific to this specialty was confirmed this session. career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: E-1→E-4 in roughly 2-3 years is the Army-wide norm; E-5 (Sergeant) board eligibility typically follows at ~4-6 years of service. No 31B-specific timeline deviation was found — treat as the general Army enlisted pattern rather than a sourced 31B-specific figure.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial term of service (commonly a 3- or 4-year first contract)
Reclassification to 31D (CID Special Agent) after 2-10 years of service — see the separate CID record in this file
Mid-career (~8-10 years) reenlistment tied to promotion/NCO development school completion

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years — there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, a service member keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) — all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; law enforcement/security carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No law-enforcement/security-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session — treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify current-branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve law-enforcement/security billets as to any other specialty.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted, so law-enforcement/security specialists near separation are as eligible as any other MOS/rating in principle — subject to the same real gate as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json).  Whether civilian police departments, federal law-enforcement agencies, or private-security firms commonly host SkillBridge partnerships in a given service member's location was not independently verified this session — flagged as a plausible inference, not a sourced claim.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below — VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. No law-enforcement/security-specific significance beyond the general rule, though a discharge characterization below Honorable/General can also independently disqualify a candidate from many civilian and federal law-enforcement hiring processes, which typically run their own background investigation referencing the DD-214.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers artillery/armor, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act-covered theater deployment, but does NOT include a military-police/security-forces entry). Reasoned from the physical and operational nature of the job rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Musculoskeletal conditions (lower back, knees, shoulders) from years of carrying body armor, duty belt, and weapon load during extended patrol/duty shifts

Hearing loss/tinnitus from repeated firearms-range qualification and duty-weapon discharge — plausible by analogy to the combat-arms noise-exposure claims pattern in data/transition.json, but not independently confirmed for MP/security-forces specifically

Mental health conditions (PTSD, anxiety, depression) from exposure to violent incidents, detainee/corrections operations, traumatic scenes, or convoy/route-security duty during deployment — a plausible claim category for this cluster given its direct exposure to violence and law-enforcement incidents, but not pulled from a sourced VA claims-frequency dataset
To verify: check VA's published claims-by-occupation data (if available) or consult a VSO familiar with military-police/security-forces service before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No law-enforcement/security-specific eligibility variance.

example_civilian_titles: Municipal or state police officer (after academy)
Federal law-enforcement officer (with agency-specific academy, e.g., FLETC)
Private/contract security supervisor (no additional academy required)
relevant_certifications: State POST (Peace Officer Standards and Training) certification — NOT automatically conferred by MP service, see credential_gap_note
Federal Law Enforcement Training Center (FLETC) Criminal Investigator/Uniformed Police Training Program — separate academy for most federal LE agencies
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is the most consequential and most commonly misunderstood transition in this cluster: military police experience does NOT confer state POST (Peace Officer Standards and Training) certification. Nearly every civilian police job still requires the full state or municipal academy regardless of MP service. Whether MP training substitutes for any part of academy attendance is set state-by-state, not federally — some states grant partial credit for firearms qualification, defensive tactics, or legal-instruction hours toward academy requirements, but a full waiver is uncommon (Fort Leonard Wood USAMPS Reciprocity Handbook, Feb 2021, per data/transition.json). Part of why this gap exists: military police/security-forces enforcement authority is itself legally narrower than a civilian police officer's. Under the Posse Comitatus Act (18 U.S.C. §1385, extended from the Army/Air Force in 1878/1956 to also cover the Navy, Marine Corps, and Space Force in 2021), DoD military personnel — including MPs and Security Forces — have NO general federal criminal-law-enforcement authority over civilians off a military installation. Their authority is limited to enforcing military justice (UCMJ) over persons subject to it and to force-protection/access-control of DoD property. A person leaving this career field should expect to attend an abbreviated or, in many states, a full state/municipal police academy before certification — not treat MP service as equivalent to having already been a police officer.
civilian_crosswalk.source: https://www.bls.gov/ooh/protective-service/police-and-detectives.htm ; https://www.bls.gov/ooh/protective-service/security-guards.htm ; data/transition.json (credential_gap.examples, Military Police entry, this project)

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count — drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment — a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt — not by specialty. A law-enforcement/security specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating/AFSC.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure — e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend — scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file. Coast Guard's Tuition Assistance is DHS-funded rather than DoD-funded even where the dollar caps happen to match.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army Credentialing Assistance (CA)/Army COOL: reduced to a $2,000/fiscal-year cap in 2024, lifetime limit reported at 3 credentials per 10 years of service; commissioned officers reported ineligible. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — cool.osd.mil could not be independently fetched this session. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: Army COOL lists 31B credential pathways (per cool.osd.mil, search-corroborated); no dedicated civilian police-academy co-op/apprenticeship program was found this session. The real 'bridge' mechanism for this cluster is state-level MP-to-academy reciprocity credit (see credential_gap_note), not a formal DoD apprenticeship.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: MPs deploy with supported units for law-and-order operations, convoy/route security, and detainee operations, and staff garrison provost-marshal/patrol functions (shift-based policing) between deployments. No sourced deployment-frequency figure specific to 31B was found this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Garrison MP duty involves rotating shift work (including nights/weekends) typical of policing, which affects family scheduling differently than a standard duty day; deployment tempo follows the supported unit's operational tempo.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: In-service reclassification path: Skilled Technical (ST) ≥ 107 and General Technical (GT) ≥ 110, per operationmilitarykids.org's 2026 CID guide (single-source this session, not independently cross-confirmed against a primary cid.army.mil page).
entry_requirements.education: In-service reclassification path: 60+ semester hours from an accredited institution, plus completion of the Basic Leader Course (BLC). Direct-accession path (CID Direct Accessions Pilot Program, for civilians): bachelor's degree with GPA ≥ 3.0 in a qualifying field — Criminal Justice, Forensic Science, Computer Science, Computer/Digital Forensics, Legal Studies, Accounting, Finance, Psychology, or Biology.
entry_requirements.age_range: Direct-accession pilot path: 21+ (operationmilitarykids.org). In-service reclassification path: candidate must already be a serving soldier with 2-10 years of service and SPC-SGT(P) rank — the general Army enlisted age range (17-42, data/branch-profiles.json) applied at original accession, not a 31D-specific ceiling.
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Army medical/fitness standards for in-service soldiers reclassifying; direct-accession applicants complete standard Army enlistment physical/fitness processing.
entry_requirements.security_clearance: Single Scope Background Investigation (SSBI) required — a more extensive investigation than a standard Secret clearance, reflecting the felony-investigation and plainclothes/undercover nature of the role.
entry_requirements.other: 31D is NOT a MEPS-selectable entry-level MOS for most applicants. It is an accession-controlled specialty reached either by in-service reclassification (2-10 years of service, 'suitable character, integrity, reputation, sobriety, discretion, and stability' per SSBI) or via the CID Direct Accessions Pilot Program for qualifying civilian degree-holders. U.S. citizenship required for both paths.

training_pipeline.description: Successful CID applicants must complete 31B MP OSUT as a foundation before CID-specific training, per operationmilitarykids.org. training_pipeline.description: Covers investigative law, evidence collection and chain of custody, interview and interrogation techniques, forensic fundamentals, and courtroom-testimony preparation, per operationmilitarykids.org and battalionduty.com.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: CID investigative and forensic experience is a genuine, well-regarded credential in federal law-enforcement hiring — CID background is commonly cited as an asset in FBI/DEA/ATF/Secret Service and other federal agency applications. But it does NOT waive the requirement: nearly every federal law-enforcement agency still requires its own academy (most commonly FLETC's Criminal Investigator Training Program, or an agency-specific academy like the FBI Academy) regardless of CID experience, and a state/municipal detective role still requires state POST certification, which is set state-by-state and not automatically conferred by military CID service. The 'combined seamless transfer' assumption is the credential-gap risk here, same as for 31B MP — the difference is that CID experience is a stronger resume differentiator, not that the academy requirement disappears.

education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file. Coast Guard's Tuition Assistance is DHS-funded rather than DoD-funded even where the dollar caps happen to match.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army Credentialing Assistance (CA)/Army COOL: reduced to a $2,000/fiscal-year cap in 2024, lifetime limit reported at 3 credentials per 10 years of service; commissioned officers reported ineligible. deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: CID assignments are garrison/field-office based rather than combat-arms-unit based, which some sources describe as offering more assignment stability than a line MP unit, but this was not independently confirmed this session.

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: E-1→E-4 in roughly 2-3 years and E-5 board eligibility at ~4-6 years is the Navy-wide enlisted norm; no MA-specific timeline deviation was found this session.

career_progression.officer_conversion_path: Limited Duty Officer (LDO) or Chief Warrant Officer (CWO) security/law-enforcement designator — general Navy LDO/CWO pathway pattern; MA-specific conversion mechanics were not independently confirmed this session.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years — there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, a service member keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) — all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; law enforcement/security carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No law-enforcement/security-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session — treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify current-branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve law-enforcement/security billets as to any other specialty.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted, so law-enforcement/security specialists near separation are as eligible as any other MOS/rating in principle — subject to the same real gate as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json). Master-at-Arms sailors with NCIS support-billet or expeditionary security force experience may have an additional differentiator for federal law-enforcement SkillBridge placements, though this was not independently confirmed this session. Whether civilian police departments, federal law-enforcement agencies, or private-security firms commonly host SkillBridge partnerships in a given service member's location was not independently verified this session — flagged as a plausible inference, not a sourced claim.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below — VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. No law-enforcement/security-specific significance beyond the general rule, though a discharge characterization below Honorable/General can also independently disqualify a candidate from many civilian and federal law-enforcement hiring processes, which typically run their own background investigation referencing the DD-214.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers artillery/armor, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act-covered theater deployment, but does NOT include a military-police/security-forces entry). Reasoned from the physical and operational nature of the job rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Musculoskeletal conditions (lower back, knees, shoulders) from years of carrying body armor, duty belt, and weapon load during extended patrol/duty shifts

Hearing loss/tinnitus from repeated firearms-range qualification and duty-weapon discharge — plausible by analogy to the combat-arms noise-exposure claims pattern in data/transition.json, but not independently confirmed for MP/security-forces specifically

Mental health conditions (PTSD, anxiety, depression) from exposure to violent incidents, detainee/corrections operations, traumatic scenes, or convoy/route-security duty during deployment — a plausible claim category for this cluster given its direct exposure to violence and law-enforcement incidents, but not pulled from a sourced VA claims-frequency dataset
To verify: check VA's published claims-by-occupation data (if available) or consult a VSO familiar with military-police/security-forces service before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No law-enforcement/security-specific eligibility variance.

civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is the most consequential and most commonly misunderstood transition in this cluster: military police experience does NOT confer state POST (Peace Officer Standards and Training) certification. Nearly every civilian police job still requires the full state or municipal academy regardless of MP service. Whether MP training substitutes for any part of academy attendance is set state-by-state, not federally — some states grant partial credit for firearms qualification, defensive tactics, or legal-instruction hours toward academy requirements, but a full waiver is uncommon (Fort Leonard Wood USAMPS Reciprocity Handbook, Feb 2021, per data/transition.json). Part of why this gap exists: military police/security-forces enforcement authority is itself legally narrower than a civilian police officer's. Under the Posse Comitatus Act (18 U.S.C. §1385, extended from the Army/Air Force in 1878/1956 to also cover the Navy, Marine Corps, and Space Force in 2021), DoD military personnel — including MPs and Security Forces — have NO general federal criminal-law-enforcement authority over civilians off a military installation. Their authority is limited to enforcing military justice (UCMJ) over persons subject to it and to force-protection/access-control of DoD property. A person leaving this career field should expect to attend an abbreviated or, in many states, a full state/municipal police academy before certification — not treat MP service as equivalent to having already been a police officer.

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count — drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment — a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt — not by specialty. A law-enforcement/security specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating/AFSC.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure — e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

(data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file. Coast Guard's Tuition Assistance is DHS-funded rather than DoD-funded even where the dollar caps happen to match.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Navy Credentialing Opportunities On-Line (COOL) funds credential exams/renewals/fees for active-duty Navy and Navy Reserve (excluding IRR/VTU); a specific dollar cap was NOT confirmed this session (cool.osd.mil fetch failed with a certificate error). education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: No formal civilian police-academy co-op/apprenticeship program was found this session for the MA rating. Some MAs pursue NCIS-adjacent investigative experience, which may be a resume differentiator for federal investigative-agency hiring, but this is not a credentialing pathway per se.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: MAs deploy for force-protection duty aboard ships, with expeditionary security force (ESF) units, and on individual-augmentee assignments to high-threat environments, in addition to standard installation-security and law-enforcement duty ashore. No MA-specific deployment-frequency figure was independently sourced this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Sea-duty and shore-duty rotations follow the standard Navy pattern; ESF and individual-augmentee assignments can involve deployment separations comparable to other Navy expeditionary ratings.

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years — there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, a service member keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) — all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; law enforcement/security carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No law-enforcement/security-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session — treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify current-branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve law-enforcement/security billets as to any other specialty.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted, so law-enforcement/security specialists near separation are as eligible as any other MOS/rating in principle — subject to the same real gate as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json).  Whether civilian police departments, federal law-enforcement agencies, or private-security firms commonly host SkillBridge partnerships in a given service member's location was not independently verified this session — flagged as a plausible inference, not a sourced claim.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is the most consequential and most commonly misunderstood transition in this cluster: military police experience does NOT confer state POST (Peace Officer Standards and Training) certification. Nearly every civilian police job still requires the full state or municipal academy regardless of MP service. Whether MP training substitutes for any part of academy attendance is set state-by-state, not federally — some states grant partial credit for firearms qualification, defensive tactics, or legal-instruction hours toward academy requirements, but a full waiver is uncommon (Fort Leonard Wood USAMPS Reciprocity Handbook, Feb 2021, per data/transition.json). Part of why this gap exists: military police/security-forces enforcement authority is itself legally narrower than a civilian police officer's. Under the Posse Comitatus Act (18 U.S.C. §1385, extended from the Army/Air Force in 1878/1956 to also cover the Navy, Marine Corps, and Space Force in 2021), DoD military personnel — including MPs and Security Forces — have NO general federal criminal-law-enforcement authority over civilians off a military installation. Their authority is limited to enforcing military justice (UCMJ) over persons subject to it and to force-protection/access-control of DoD property. A person leaving this career field should expect to attend an abbreviated or, in many states, a full state/municipal police academy before certification — not treat MP service as equivalent to having already been a police officer.

Federal Law Enforcement Training Center (FLETC) programs — separate academy required for most federal civilian LE agencies
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Genuine differentiator worth stating plainly: unlike the four DoD services' MP/Security Forces roles above, the Coast Guard Maritime Enforcement Specialist rating carries REAL federal civil law-enforcement authority over civilians — not just military-justice/force-protection authority. Because the Coast Guard operates under the Department of Homeland Security and is explicitly exempted from the Posse Comitatus Act (which restricts the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and Space Force from civilian law enforcement), MEs conducting vessel boardings exercise genuine federal search, seizure, and arrest authority under Title 14 and customs/maritime statutes — this is a structurally different (and broader) authority than any DoD MP/Security Forces role holds while in uniform. That said, this does NOT eliminate the credential gap on separation: this federal maritime/ boarding-officer authority does not itself confer a state POST certification for a municipal or state police department job, nor does it waive a different federal agency's own academy (e.g., FLETC's Criminal Investigator Training Program or Land Management Police Training Program) for someone pursuing, say, a Customs and Border Protection or federal-park-police career. ME experience is a strong, genuinely relevant credential in a federal law-enforcement hiring process — real arrest-authority experience is rare among enlisted veterans — but it is a resume differentiator that still requires the hiring agency's own academy, not an automatic waiver.

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count — drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment — a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt — not by specialty. A law-enforcement/security specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating/AFSC.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure — e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend — scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file. Coast Guard's Tuition Assistance is DHS-funded rather than DoD-funded even where the dollar caps happen to match.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Coast Guard Credentialing Opportunities On-Line (COOL): a search summary attributed a $4,500/year figure to the Coast Guard, but it appears to describe Coast Guard Tuition Assistance rather than Credentialing Assistance specifically — flagged as a likely conflation, not used with confidence here. UNVERIFIED. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: No formal civilian police-academy co-op/apprenticeship program was found this session for the ME rating. The genuine bridge asset for this rating is boarding-officer/LEDET experience carrying real federal arrest authority, which is a resume differentiator for federal maritime/border law-enforcement hiring — not a formal credentialing pathway.

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: MEs deploy aboard Coast Guard cutters and as embarked Law Enforcement Detachments (LEDETs) on Navy vessels for counter-narcotics and migrant-interdiction operations, and serve in port/waterways security roles ashore between deployments. No ME-specific deployment-frequency figure was independently sourced this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Cutter-based assignments involve underway/patrol rotations comparable to other sea-service ratings; Deployable Specialized Forces team selection can involve extended TDY/deployment separations beyond the standard cutter rotation.

completion_accountability: 6 of the targeted 5-7 SpecialtyRecords completed and fully populated per the OUTPUT SPEC schema: Army 31B (Military Police), Army 31D (CID Special Agent), Navy MA (Master-at-Arms), Air Force 3P0X1 (Security Forces), Marine Corps 5811 (Military Police), and Coast Guard ME (Maritime Enforcement Specialist). Space Force is correctly excluded and documented in branches_with_no_presence rather than padded, per the master prompt's explicit instruction for this cluster and data/interest-taxonomy.json's own note that Space Force installation security is provided by Air Force Security Forces. No SpecialtyRecord field was left blank — every field is either a sourced value, a cross-corroborated-but-primary-unconfirmed value (flagged), a null with an explanatory note (e.g. bonuses), or a labeled inference/guess (e.g. VA disability claims list, Army 31D's representative pay figure). The credential gap — that military police experience does NOT confer state POST certification, and that nearly every civilian police job still requires the state academy regardless of MP service — is stated plainly and without softening in every DoD-branch record's civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note (army-31b, army-31d [extended to the federal-academy analog], navy-ma, air-force-3p0x1, marine-corps-5811), and is explicitly tied to the legal reason it exists: the Posse Comitatus Act (18 U.S.C. §1385, extended to the Navy/Marine Corps/Space Force in 2021) strips DoD military police/security-forces personnel of general civil law-enforcement authority over civilians, so their training is legally narrower than a civilian police officer's from the outset. The Coast Guard Maritime Enforcement Specialist record states the genuine differentiator required by the task: because the Coast Guard is a DHS component explicitly exempted from the Posse Comitatus Act, MEs hold REAL federal civil law-enforcement authority (search, seizure, arrest under Title 14 and customs/maritime statutes) during service — structurally broader than any DoD MP/Security Forces role — while still being honest that this does not itself waive a state POST academy or a different federal agency's own academy on separation. Tool usage: 10 WebSearch calls against the ~10-call budget stated for this command (Army 31B, Army 31D, Navy MA, Air Force 3P0X1, Marine Corps 5811, Coast Guard ME requirements/training; Posse Comitatus Act and Coast Guard exception; BLS wage data; Army/Air Force bonus context; a supplementary BLS SOC-code check), then research was stopped per the binding budget instruction and the file was written from that research plus this project's existing data/pay-tables.json, data/retirement.json, data/education-benefits.json, data/transition.json, and data/veteran-benefits.json. Several official .mil pages (mynavyhr.navy.mil, static.e-publishing.af.mil's CFETP PDF, cool.osd.mil) returned HTTP 403 or certificate errors and were not re-attempted, consistent with the binding instruction not to burn calls chasing a 403 — each such gap is flagged in the affected record and in the unverified list above rather than silently filled with an invented figure. No dollar figure, ASVAB score, training length, or URL was fabricated; every field traces to a named source with a retrieval date, or is explicitly labeled UNVERIFIED or a reasoned inference.
